fundamental_rights: Article 21 — Right to Life with dignity (includes right against cruelty in marriage)
Article 14 — Right to Equality (applies to matrimonial proceedings)
Article 15 — No discrimination on grounds of religion/sex in personal laws subject to reform

laws.name: Hindu Marriage Act, 1955
sections: Section 13 (grounds for divorce — adultery, cruelty, desertion etc.)
Section 13B (divorce by mutual consent — 1 year separation minimum)
laws.summary: Governs marriage, divorce, maintenance, and child custody for Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, and Jains.

laws.name: Special Marriage Act, 1954
laws.summary: Allows marriage between any two persons regardless of religion. Also provides divorce provisions.
laws.remedy: File petition in Family Court or District Court

laws.name: Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 2019
laws.summary: Triple talaq (instant divorce) is unconstitutional and a criminal offence. Husband can be imprisoned.
laws.remedy: File complaint with police or Magistrate

laws.name: Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 / Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956
laws.summary: Welfare of the child is the paramount consideration in custody disputes.
laws.remedy: File custody petition in Family Court

laws.name: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) / IPC
laws.summary: Bigamy is a criminal offence. Cruelty in marriage is punishable with imprisonment.
laws.remedy: File FIR for bigamy or cruelty

laws.name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
laws.summary: Provides civil remedies in addition to criminal provisions for married women facing violence.
laws.remedy: Approach Protection Officer or file application in Magistrate Court

steps: For divorce: consult a family lawyer; if mutual consent — both parties can file jointly after 1 year separation
For maintenance: file application under Section 125 CrPC / BNSS in Family Court — interim maintenance can be ordered within 60 days
For child custody: file petition in Family Court — the court considers the child's welfare above all else
For domestic violence: contact Protection Officer and file application in Magistrate Court
Gather evidence: marriage certificate, children's documents, financial records, bank statements
For free legal aid: contact DLSA (District Legal Services Authority) or NALSA 15100
Consider mediation — Family Courts often refer parties to mediation centers first
